regions.us-ports: Today at Charleston and nearby US East Coast ports including Savannah, Norfolk, and Jacksonville, winds are light from the north-northeast at 4 knots with clear skies and zero percent rain chance, allowing smooth embarkation and disembarkation operations and no interruptions to shore excursions. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, expect a shift to southerly winds near 12 knots with overcast skies and minimal rain chances of 1 to 2 percent, affecting port operations from Charleston through Savannah and potentially as far south as Miami and Port Everglades with mostly dry conditions and steady visibility. Ports along the Gulf Coast such as Galveston, Houston, and New Orleans will also experience southerly winds near 12 to 13 knots with overcast skies but very low rain probabilities, maintaining stable docking and tendering conditions. Beyond 48 hours, southerly winds will persist around 13 knots with intermittent overcast conditions and rain chances remaining below 2 percent, so cruise passengers should anticipate consistent sea and sky conditions with no expected weather-related delays in the US Ports region through April 24. Overall, no marine wind warnings or rain impacts are forecast that would affect cruise operations or shore excursions at this time.
regions.bahamas-central-caribbean: Today, conditions in Nassau feature northeast winds at 7 knots with clear skies and zero percent chance of rain, supporting smooth port operations and uninterrupted shore excursions across the Bahamas, including Freeport, Bimini, and the Berry Islands. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, expect east-northeast winds increasing to 14-15 knots with overcast skies and a slight rise in rain probability to 3-10 percent, which may cause minor visibility reductions during embarkation and disembarkation at Nassau, Key West, and Grand Cayman. By 48 hours, east-southeast winds will hold steady near 15 knots accompanied by drizzle and a 23 percent chance of rain, potentially affecting cruise operations and shore activities in Ocho Rios, Falmouth, Puerto Plata, La Romana, Santo Domingo, and Samaná with damp conditions and lighter seas. No marine wind advisories are currently in effect, allowing for routine vessel maneuvering within all listed ports. Passengers and crew should monitor evolving sky conditions as drizzle and increased cloud cover may require adjustments to outdoor plans beyond Day 2.
regions.eastern-caribbean: Today we have a PRIORITY ALERT for thunderstorms with a 53% rain probability expected at San Juan, St. Thomas, and St. Croix, which will likely impact port operations and shore excursions by late afternoon as winds increase to 11 knots from the east under overcast skies. Currently, San Juan conditions are southeast at 4 knots with partly cloudy skies and only a 5% chance of rain, allowing normal embarkation and disembarkation this morning. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, expect easterly winds steady around 10 knots with increasing cloud cover and rain chances rising to 33% in San Juan and St. Maarten, which may cause delays or cancellations for some outdoor shore activities. By day three, thunderstorms with a 53% rain chance and east winds at 11 knots will affect Antigua and St. Kitts as well, requiring heightened readiness for vessel maneuvering and passenger safety. Beyond 48 hours, persistently overcast skies and sustained east-northeast winds near 10 knots suggest continued wet conditions across all Eastern Caribbean ports, so passengers and crew should anticipate ongoing impacts to outdoor operations.
regions.western-caribbean: Today, easterly winds at 8 to 9 knots combined with partly cloudy skies and a 40% chance of rain showers will affect Cozumel and nearby ports including Costa Maya and Belize City, potentially causing intermittent wet conditions during embarkation and some shore excursions. In the next 24 to 48 hours, increasing rain probabilities up to 65% with east-southeast winds at 9 knots will impact Cozumel, Roatan, and Belize City, possibly delaying outdoor activities and requiring adjustments to port operations. Wind conditions remain moderate with no small craft advisories, but crews should monitor for localized showers and reduced visibility in these areas. Beyond 48 hours, winds shift to south-southeast at 8 knots with partly cloudy skies and a lower 20% rain chance expected in Cozumel, suggesting improving conditions for port calls and excursions across the western Caribbean. Passengers and crew should remain prepared for occasional showers through tomorrow, especially in Cozumel and Belize City, while expecting better weather by April 24th.
regions.southern-caribbean: Today in Aruba, winds are steady from the east-northeast at 11 knots under an overcast sky with only a 5 percent chance of rain, allowing normal port operations and shore excursions to proceed with minimal weather impact. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, expect winds to shift slightly more easterly, increasing to 13 to 14 knots with persistent overcast conditions and elevated rain chances up to 33 percent on day one, particularly affecting Aruba and Curacao’s port operations and excursion schedules due to occasional light showers. By the second day, rain chances decrease to 15 percent with continued easterly 14-knot winds, improving conditions for Cartagena and Bonaire, where embarkation and disembarkation should remain largely unaffected. Beyond 48 hours, winds remain easterly near 13 knots with overcast skies and rain probability dropping to 8 percent, signaling a trend toward drier conditions that will support smoother cruise ship operations across all Southern Caribbean ports. Passengers and crew should remain prepared for intermittent cloud cover but can anticipate minimal weather disruptions moving forward.
regions.lesser-antilles: PRIORITY ALERT — Moderate rain showers will begin impacting port operations and shore excursions in the Lesser Antilles on Day 3 with a 35% rain probability and east winds around 10 knots. Today, April 22, Barbados experiences ENE winds at 9 knots with partly cloudy skies and only a 3% chance of rain, allowing normal embarkation, disembarkation, and shore activities with minimal weather disruption. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, from April 23 to 24, expect overcast skies and a 30% chance of rain with east winds steady at 9 knots affecting Barbados, St. Lucia, Martinique, and Dominica, which may cause localized wet conditions during shore excursions but no significant operational delays. Beyond 48 hours, on April 25, rain showers increase to a 35% probability with E winds at 10 knots, most notably impacting Barbados, Antigua, St. Kitts, and St. Maarten, where intermittent showers could reduce visibility and require flexible scheduling of outdoor activities and tender operations. Crew and passengers should monitor updates for evolving rain patterns and maintain caution for slick surfaces during embarkation and excursions in the affected ports.
regions.los-angeles: Today, winds out of the west-northwest at 6 knots combined with clear skies and a 0% chance of rain support smooth embarkation and disembarkation at Los Angeles, San Pedro, Long Beach, and Marina del Rey, with shore excursions to Catalina Island proceeding without weather disruptions. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, increasing west-northwest winds to 13 knots and partly cloudy skies with just a 1% rain chance on day one will slightly elevate sea conditions around San Pedro and Long Beach but remain safe for all port operations. By day two, sustained west winds near 12 knots and overcast skies with no rain expected will affect all ports, including Catalina Island, requiring crews to monitor visibility for tender operations. Beyond 48 hours, winds shift to a south-southeast direction at 11 knots with continued overcast and zero rain probability, signaling a steady but manageable change in sea state that passengers and crew should prepare for during transit and shore excursions. No marine wind warnings or precipitation hazards are forecasted, so cruise schedules should remain on track throughout this period.
regions.ensenada: Today in the Ensenada region, winds are light at 1 knot from the northwest with overcast skies and only a 3% chance of rain, allowing normal port operations and shore excursions in Ensenada, Punta Banda, and Islas Todos Santos to proceed without weather interruptions. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, expect increasing winds from the west-northwest at 17 knots on Day 1 and then westerly winds at 13 knots on Day 2, with continued overcast conditions and minimal rain chances of 4% and 0% respectively, which may cause moderate chop affecting tender operations primarily at Punta Banda and Islas Todos Santos. Beyond 48 hours, winds will ease slightly to 10 knots from the west on Day 3 with persistent overcast skies and no rain expected, improving sea state for safer embarkation and disembarkation in all three ports. Keep in mind that the sustained wind speeds forecasted do not trigger any Small Craft or Gale Warnings, so cruise operations should remain stable throughout the period.
regions.cabo-san-lucas: Today, winds out of the north-northwest at 15 knots will keep conditions steady across Cabo San Lucas with partly cloudy skies and zero chance of rain, supporting smooth embarkation and disembarkation operations at all three ports. Shore excursions in Cabo San Lucas, San Jose del Cabo, and La Paz will proceed without weather-related interruptions due to stable wind speeds and clear visibility. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, northwest winds will persist between 14 and 16 knots with continued partly cloudy skies and no rain expected, maintaining favorable sea states for vessel maneuvering and passenger comfort in all ports. Beyond 48 hours, no significant changes are forecast as winds remain consistently from the northwest near 16 knots with clear skies and no precipitation, allowing uninterrupted cruise operations and onshore activities through La Paz and the southern Baja region. Passengers and crew should anticipate steady marine conditions with no weather hazards impacting the Cabo San Lucas region through this period.
regions.mazatlan: Today in the Mazatlan region, winds are light from the south-southwest at 2 knots with clear skies and zero chance of rain, providing excellent conditions for all port operations and shore excursions at Mazatlan, Topolobampo, and Altata. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, expect west-southwest winds increasing to 9 knots with overcast skies on day one, transitioning to westerly winds around 7 to 8 knots with partly cloudy skies on days two and three; these conditions will be consistent across all three ports with no rain expected, ensuring smooth embarkation and disembarkation activities. Beyond 48 hours, the steady westerly winds between 7 and 8 knots combined with partly cloudy skies and continued zero percent rain probability indicate stable weather for ongoing cruise operations in Mazatlan, Topolobampo, and Altata. There are no marine wind warnings or weather hazards forecasted, so vessel maneuvering and shore excursions should proceed without weather-related disruption.
regions.puerto-vallarta: Today in the Puerto Vallarta region, winds are light from the northwest at 3 knots with partly cloudy skies and zero percent chance of rain, supporting smooth embarkation and shore excursions at Puerto Vallarta, Punta Mita, Yelapa, and Chacala. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, expect increasing wester-northwest winds steady at 8 knots with overcast conditions and a minimal 3 percent rain chance on Day 1, which may slightly reduce visibility and cause minor impacts to tender operations especially at the more exposed Yelapa and Chacala ports. By Day 2, skies will clear to partly cloudy while winds remain from the WNW at 8 knots with rain chances holding at 3 percent, allowing near-normal operations across all ports. Beyond 48 hours on Day 3, wester-northwest winds steady at 8 knots and zero percent rain probability will favor calm seas and excellent conditions for all cruise vessel maneuvers and shore excursions in the region. No marine wind advisories or weather warnings are currently forecast, so operations should proceed with standard safety protocols.
regions.western-mediterranean: Today in the Western Mediterranean, winds are calm out of the north-northwest at 0 knots in Barcelona with partly cloudy skies and zero percent chance of rain, allowing smooth port operations and shore excursions in Barcelona, Valencia, Palma de Mallorca, and Ibiza. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, expect increasing easterly winds reaching 14 knots from the east-northeast by day two, with overcast skies and a 28 percent chance of rain impacting port activities primarily in Barcelona and nearby coastal stops such as Palma de Mallorca and Ibiza, where some outdoor excursions may experience brief disruptions. Beyond 48 hours, conditions ease with winds dropping back to 8 knots from the east-southeast and skies becoming partly cloudy again, indicating improved visibility and calmer seas for ports including Malaga, Cadiz, Lisbon, and Gibraltar, facilitating smoother embarkation and disembarkation processes. regions.central-mediterranean: Today in Naples and across nearby Central Mediterranean ports including Civitavecchia, Livorno, and La Spezia, expect northeast winds around 7 knots with overcast skies and only a 3% chance of rain, supporting smooth embarkation and shore excursions with minimal weather disruption. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, wind speeds will increase slightly to 11-12 knots from the northeast while skies remain overcast, maintaining stable conditions for port operations in Naples, Genoa, and Nice with rain probabilities holding at or below 5 percent. By day three, winds will shift to north-northwest at 9 knots with partly cloudy skies and zero rain chance, improving visibility and likely easing ship maneuvering in Venice, Split, and Dubrovnik. No advisory-level wind events or rain threats are forecast, so cruise schedules and tender operations should proceed without significant weather-related interruptions across Sardinia and Corsica. Passengers and crew should monitor the wind direction shift beyond 48 hours as it may affect vessel docking angles and minor sea state changes in eastern Adriatic ports.
regions.eastern-mediterranean: Today, expect overcast skies with a 50% chance of rain and east winds at 8 knots around Athens (Piraeus), Santorini, and Mykonos, which may cause delays in shore excursions and some operational adjustments at these ports. Ports including Rhodes, Izmir, and Cyprus should prepare for increasing cloud cover and similar rain probabilities as vessels approach later today. Over the next 24 to 48 hours, northeast winds at 6 knots combined with a 43% chance of rain will impact Athens and nearby ports such as Santorini and Mykonos, potentially affecting embarkation and disembarkation schedules. Corfu, Istanbul, Haifa, Alexandria, and Rhodes will see lighter winds but should monitor conditions as northeast flow persists with overcast skies and intermittent rain. Beyond 48 hours, skies will remain mostly overcast with a reduced 5% rain chance and northeast winds increasing to 9 knots at Athens, indicating improving conditions for port operations throughout the Eastern Mediterranean.
